jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana riff on 'BTS' escalating from 'behind the scenes' to sexual innuendo: 'I love a hot BTS' / 'BTS to the BTS, baby'
jokes.setup: The two are doing a straight promotional segment about behind-the-scenes footage, using the abbreviation 'BTS'
jokes.punchline: They immediately start playing with the double meaning, escalating to 'I love a hot BTS' — turning a bland promo into a dirty aside
jokes.explanation: Functional wordplay that fits the Abbi/Ilana dynamic of making everything vaguely sexual. The escalation from 'I love a good BTS' to 'I love a hot BTS' is quick and efficient. Not inspired, but it's a warm-up joke that signals the show's register before the episode even starts.

jokes.text: Bobbi calls the driver 'Driver' and Eliot corrects her: 'Mom, don't call him driver.' Bobbi's response: 'Yeah, they call this security, this guy. Like, he's gonna stop anybody, huh?'
jokes.setup: Eliot politely corrects his mother for calling the security guard 'driver,' implying she's being rude/entitled
jokes.punchline: Bobbi pivots immediately to mocking the security guard's competence rather than apologizing — doubling down on her obliviousness with a withering physical assessment
jokes.explanation: Bobbi's character is established instantly — she doesn't hear corrections, she hears jumping-off points for new complaints. The move from social faux pas to dismissive physical judgment is fast and character-specific. Carol Kane's delivery presumably sells it.

jokes.text: The gate security asks 'Esther Baumgarten?' and Bobbi snaps back 'No. No, that's my mother. She's dead.' Then shouts 'Dead! She's dead!' when the guard doesn't immediately respond
jokes.setup: Routine security name check at a gated Florida community
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's grief/irritation fuse together into aggressive over-clarification — she shouts 'Dead! She's dead!' as if the man is questioning her mother's mortality
jokes.explanation: Great little character moment. The joke layers grief, irritability, and entitlement into a single overreaction. The repetition ('No. No... Dead! She's dead!') feels authentically unhinged in a very specific New York Jewish mom way. Economy is excellent — maximum character with minimum words.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'God, I needed to be slapped in the face with this Vitamin D. I could feel my mustache lightening.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi steps into the Florida sunshine and expresses relief
jokes.punchline: 'I could feel my mustache lightening' — the specificity of this detail (a mustache that is sun-bleached, mentioned with zero self-consciousness) is the whole joke
jokes.explanation: The mustache detail is pitch-perfect character writing. It's not a setup/punchline joke — it's pure character revelation delivered as mundane observation. Bobbi mentions her facial hair the way someone else would mention their sinuses. The specificity ('lightening,' not 'bleaching') and complete lack of shame make this land hard. High character integration score because only this character would say this without irony.

jokes.text: Eliot deadpans: 'Yup, you told us and the TSA guy.' — in response to Bobbi's mustache comment
jokes.setup: Bobbi has just said something deeply personal/oversharing about her mustache
jokes.punchline: Eliot's flat confirmation that she also told a stranger at airport security — implying this is a pattern and the TSA agent had no way out
jokes.explanation: A perfect straight-man button to Bobbi's overshare. The joke is in the word 'and' — not 'even' or 'also,' just flat 'and' — treating the TSA confession as simply the prior instance in a recurring pattern. Extremely economical.

jokes.text: Eliot lost his luggage but kept insisting it was an 'amazing suitcase' from Refinery29 that Drew Barrymore recommends — and Abbi/Ilana finish his sentence for him
jokes.setup: Eliot is annoyed about lost luggage and pivots to talking up the suitcase itself
jokes.punchline: The others know him well enough to complete '...that Drew...' with 'Barrymore recommends' — the celebrity endorsement detail is so specific and so Eliot that it's been noted before
jokes.explanation: Competent character joke. The Drew Barrymore name-drop works because it's specific without being random — it characterizes Eliot as someone who reads Refinery29 and treats celebrity lifestyle content earnestly. The chorus finishing his sentence is a nice touch of established shorthand.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'It's insane that didn't do carry-on. But, look, I had to check. I had my shampoo and my conditioner. And it's made especially for the humid...ity.' Pauses on 'humid' before finishing 'ity'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is defending her choice to check luggage that subsequently got lost
jokes.punchline: The conspicuous pause before '-ity' — she's clearly about to say something else or stops herself, then awkwardly tacks on the suffix. The joke is the word she didn't say.
jokes.explanation: A small moment that rewards attention. The humor is entirely in delivery — Bobbi presumably was going to say something coarser or more honest ('humid-ness' of her hair situation vs. something more personal) and stops. The transcript captures the pause orthographically. Rewatch bonus because you catch it better the second time.

jokes.text: Ilana delivers the Florida welcome: 'America's droopy dick.'
jokes.setup: The family arrives in Florida; Ilana surveys the geography/state
jokes.punchline: Florida's peninsular shape reframed as 'America's droopy dick' — a visual observation delivered as a tourism-brochure welcome
jokes.explanation: Four words that do a lot of work. 'Droopy' specifically — not just 'dick,' which would be crude, but 'droopy dick,' which implies aging, dysfunction, and Florida's general vibe simultaneously. Maximum economy. The word 'droopy' is doing heavy lifting. Feels like a line that was actually in the writers' room, not improvised.

jokes.text: Bobbi tells Abbi she needs to change her Facebook profile picture: 'You're not doing yourself any favors.' Then reveals she's Facebook friends with ALL of Ilana's friends.
jokes.setup: Bobbi has unsolicited advice for Abbi about her Facebook photo
jokes.punchline: Ilana: 'Why are you guys even friends on Facebook?' Bobbi: 'I'm friends with all your friends on Facebook. And I got news for you. They request me.' — the revelation that Bobbi has infiltrated her daughter's entire social network
jokes.explanation: Bobbi's clarification that her daughter's friends request HER ('they request me') is the best beat — it inverts the expected dynamic (Bobbi adding people) and implies Bobbi is somehow cooler or more compelling than her own daughter on social media. Abbi's 'She's right, I mean, I did. A couple of times, actually.' button is excellent.

jokes.text: Abbi admits she sent Bobbi Facebook friend requests 'a couple of times, actually'
jokes.setup: Ilana pushes back on Bobbi being friends with her friends on Facebook
jokes.punchline: Abbi quietly confirms she not only sent Bobbi a request, but did it multiple times — the 'actually' and the pause sell the embarrassment
jokes.explanation: The 'couple of times' escalation is key — it's not just that she added Bobbi once (forgivable), it's that Bobbi apparently declined and Abbi tried again. Efficient character moment for Abbi: slightly desperate for approval from maternal figures.

jokes.text: Eleanor shoots a gun at the gate in greeting — and then Ilana and Eliot react with 'Ahh!' / '[BLEEP] Florida.'
jokes.setup: Warm reunion at the gate of the Florida retirement community; hugs being exchanged
jokes.punchline: A gunshot punctuates the group hug. Eliot's '[BLEEP] Florida' is the button.
jokes.explanation: A solid visual/audio shock gag. The gun as celebratory greeting is absurd but also a specific, credible Florida joke. '[BLEEP] Florida' as a reaction achieves economy — it's not just the moment, it's the accumulated frustration of the entire trip crystallized into two words.

jokes.text: Bev brags about SoulCycle making her sweat — Bobbi immediately deadpans: 'Otherwise known as a hot flash.'
jokes.setup: Bev attributes her sweating to SoulCycle from a month ago, positioning herself as athletic and vital
jokes.punchline: Bobbi reduces the whole claim to menopause in three words
jokes.explanation: One of the tighter jokes in the episode. The setup (Bev's self-congratulatory fitness claim) is efficient, and Bobbi's demolition of it is ruthlessly economical. 'Otherwise known as a hot flash' works because it doesn't over-explain — it assumes the audience is smart enough to connect sweating/SoulCycle/menopause. The sibling rivalry dynamic is also established instantly. Close to an 8 in craft.

jokes.text: Bev inspects Abbi's nails: 'What is that, "Trophy Wife"?' Abbi: 'Second wife.'
jokes.setup: Bev identifies a nail polish shade by name, guessing the brand/shade
jokes.punchline: Abbi corrects her to a slightly more degrading category — not a trophy wife, but a second wife. The distinction says everything about Abbi's self-image.
jokes.explanation: Two words ('Second wife.') that do a tremendous amount of character work. The joke operates on multiple levels: (1) it's a real OPI shade name, giving it specificity; (2) Abbi's self-deprecating correction implies she doesn't see herself as a trophy — she's the replacement model; (3) it's delivered flat, which is perfect. High economy score because the ratio of words to comedy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Bobbi sniffs Bev and hisses 'She smells of mothballs' — launching the sisters into a fight about who started cleaning out the apartment first
jokes.setup: Sisters reuniting ostensibly to clean out their mother's Florida apartment together
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's sniff-and-accuse move — the mothball smell as forensic evidence of pre-cleaning — is both absurd and deeply believable for these characters
jokes.explanation: The sniff as detective move is funny and very specific. The escalation into the Hamilton-tickets grievance gives the sisterly conflict real texture — these are women who have been keeping score for decades.

jokes.text: Bobbi invokes her diaphragm-insertion teachings as grounds for respect in the argument: 'I'm the person who taught you how to insert a diaphragm!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi and Bev are screaming at each other about the estate; Bobbi tells Bev not to raise her voice
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's assertion of maternal authority via diaphragm instruction — a completely unexpected credential to invoke mid-argument
jokes.explanation: This is the best Bobbi line in the episode. 'I'm the person who taught you how to insert a diaphragm!' as a demand for respect is perfectly unexpected and yet completely consistent with this character — a woman who is simultaneously sex-positive and domineering, who treats gynecological tutorials as acts of maternal love that earn permanent deference. The non-sequitur logic ('therefore show respect') is airtight within Bobbi's worldview. High originality — this specific credential is not a cliché. Comparable to the Snip Snap anchor in terms of the unexpected physical escalation that is also character-perfect.

jokes.text: Eliot has to wear his dead grandmother's bathing suit because his luggage is lost — discovers it's Grandma Esther's, not Grandpa's
jokes.setup: Eliot can't find a bathing suit; Ilana produces one and says it was Grandpa's
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals: 'Grandpa? That's Grandma Esther's suit.' Then: 'I knew it would fit you, same hips.'
jokes.explanation: Two separate jokes in quick succession: the bait-and-switch (grandpa/grandma), then the 'same hips' kicker which is more personal and cuts deeper. Ilana's delivery of 'same hips' as though it's a compliment is key to why this works — she means it affectionately but it's mortifying. The episode has been building Eliot as the straight man who keeps getting indignities heaped on him.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I knew it would fit you, same hips.'
jokes.setup: Eliot has just discovered he's wearing Grandma Esther's swimsuit
jokes.punchline: Ilana matter-of-factly reveals she already knew and chose this suit for him because of his shared hip width with their deceased grandmother
jokes.explanation: The punchline extension works — Ilana's 'same hips' is delivered as if it's a compliment or at least a neutral fact, which makes it funnier. The casualness is the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana to Eliot in dead grandmother's bathing suit: 'Oh, own it, boi.'
jokes.setup: Eliot has declared the situation humiliating
jokes.punchline: Ilana's instant, breezy 'own it, boi' — treating a man wearing his dead grandmother's swimsuit as simply a confidence issue
jokes.explanation: The economy is excellent — three words doing character work for both Eliot (mortified) and Ilana (breezy, sex-positive, body-positive). 'Boi' specifically signals Ilana's internet-fluent patois and her genuine affection for Eliot's androgynous energy.

jokes.text: Abbi's 'solid tan plan': 'First I do my back, then I do my front. I didn't say it was, like, a complicated plan.'
jokes.setup: Abbi announces she has 'a solid tan plan' with confidence
jokes.punchline: The plan is simply front and back. She anticipates and preempts the obvious objection ('I didn't say it was complicated') which is funnier than if she'd just described the plan
jokes.explanation: Abbi's self-aware non-defense is the comedy. The joke is about her need to frame the simplest thing as an achievement, which is core Abbi. Preemptively defending an obviously basic plan is funnier than just stating it.

jokes.text: Abbi asks for help with 100 SPF sunscreen because a dermatologist called her 'devastatingly white'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains why she needs such high SPF
jokes.punchline: 'Devastatingly white' as a clinical diagnosis — the word 'devastatingly' implies catastrophe, as if the doctor was shaken by what they saw
jokes.explanation: The adverb 'devastatingly' is the joke. A doctor using dramatic language for a skin tone observation implies Abbi's paleness is so extreme it warrants grave concern. It's also self-aware (Abbi Jacobson is famously very pale) making it character-integrated without being lazy self-deprecation.

jokes.text: Ilana's reaction to being asked to apply sunscreen to Abbi: 'What is it, my [BLEEP] birthday?'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks Ilana to help apply sunscreen
jokes.punchline: Ilana treats this as an almost sexual gift — her enthusiasm for touching Abbi is a running undercurrent of the show, here brought into plain language
jokes.explanation: This is very specifically an Ilana-only line. 'What is it, my birthday?' is usually said sarcastically (to mean 'oh great, another bad thing'), but Ilana deploys it genuinely — applying sunscreen to Abbi IS a gift. The character integration score is high because this joke only works with this specific character dynamic. The bleeped word adds the appropriate emphasis.

jokes.text: Eliot's 'Too hot! Respect.' reaction to the sunscreen scene — implied he's watching Ilana apply sunscreen to Abbi in a too-enthusiastic way
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are doing something with sunscreen that is clearly getting weird/sensual
jokes.punchline: Eliot's 'Too hot! Respect.' — he's both overwhelmed and appreciative, delivered as a reaction beat rather than dialogue
jokes.explanation: A solid reaction beat. Eliot as audience surrogate for the Abbi/Ilana dynamic works here. 'Respect' as a stunned compliment is funny and in-character for a gay man appreciating a moment he wasn't expecting to see.

jokes.text: An elderly woman mistakes Ilana for the on-site mortician because she loved the eyebrow work at Doris' service
jokes.setup: A stranger approaches Ilana at the pool
jokes.punchline: She's asking not about the sunscreen situation or anything obvious — she wants to compliment the mortician's eyebrow artistry, and she thinks Ilana IS the mortician
jokes.explanation: The joke has two layers: (1) mistaking a twentysomething for a mortician and (2) the specific detail of complimenting the EYEBROW work at a funeral. The eyebrow-at-service detail is a dark, specific, and original image. Good economy — it's a quick hit before the Lincoln reveal.

jokes.text: Woman: 'Oh, you're the one dating the gangster dentist.' Ilana: 'Uh... no, we, uh... We broke up, actually.' Woman: 'Good for you.' [PAUSE]
jokes.setup: Ilana clarifies she's not the mortician, identifies herself as Esther's granddaughter. The woman immediately knows exactly who she is.
jokes.punchline: The woman's flat, enthusiastic 'Good for you' after Ilana confirms the breakup — then a long beat where Ilana presumably processes this stranger's strong opinion about Lincoln
jokes.explanation: The timestamp gap ([04:28] to [04:32] — four seconds of silence after 'Good for you') is the entire joke. An elderly Florida woman Ilana has never met has a strong opinion about Lincoln being a 'gangster dentist' and enthusiastically endorses the breakup. The silence is Ilana's reaction. The structure is excellent: the three beats (mistaken identity → dentist label → 'Good for you') escalate perfectly, and the silence lands the punchline.

jokes.text: The four-second silent beat after 'Good for you' — Ilana sitting with a stranger's enthusiastic approval of her breakup
jokes.setup: Woman has just said 'Good for you' about the Lincoln breakup
jokes.punchline: The silence itself — Ilana processing, or simply having no response to this stranger being right
jokes.explanation: A silent beat that carries emotional weight — this is actually a micro-moment where comedy and pathos merge. The silence after 'Good for you' is funny (stranger's certainty) but also slightly sad (Ilana still processing the breakup). This is the kind of subtle craft that distinguishes single-camera comedy from broad sitcom.

jokes.text: Bobbi and Bev fighting over grandmother's possessions: 'Ersatz ruby from Little Richard.' / 'Let's just cut the crap, Princess Ticklefeather.'
jokes.setup: Sisters going through their mother's things — jewelry, pearls
jokes.punchline: 'Princess Ticklefeather' as Bobbi's nickname for Bev mid-argument — specific enough to feel like it has a history, absurd enough to land as comedy
jokes.explanation: 'Princess Ticklefeather' is an excellent invented nickname — it sounds like something a sibling would have coined decades ago and never let go of. The 'Ersatz ruby from Little Richard' is also a wonderful specific detail (why does Grandma Esther have a ruby from Little Richard?) that gets dropped without explanation. Both beats suggest deep, shared history between the sisters.

jokes.text: Bev: 'So now I'm being punished because I don't have children?' [pause] 'You know, I still may.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi argues the ring should go to Ilana or Eliot (the grandchildren)
jokes.punchline: Bev's defensive pivot from victimhood ('being punished') to hope ('I still may') — the pause and the delivery imply she is not young enough for this to be a realistic option
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'You know, I still may' is everything. It's a short silence between [05:09] and [05:16] — seven seconds of processing before she decides to go with delusion over defeat. The joke is in the gap between the claim and its plausibility, and in her choosing optimism over reality. Dark without being mean.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'So now I'm being punished because I don't have children?' / [Beat] / Bev: 'Drama queen.' / [Another beat] / Bobbi: 'You know, I still may.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi argues the ring should go to her, not Ilana or Eliot
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's 'You know, I still may' — delivered after a pause about having children — implies she's delusionally convinced she could still have kids; the pause before and after lets this land
jokes.explanation: The two-beat structure (Drama queen / pause / You know, I still may) is well-timed. 'You know, I still may' implies Bobbi's age makes this unlikely but she hasn't accepted that — delivered not defensively but with stubborn confidence. Good character moment with comedic timing.

jokes.text: Ilana's New York City metaphor: 'It's like our lives are Richard Pryor's dick, and New York is the cocaine.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are basking in Florida, questioning why they live in New York
jokes.punchline: The Richard Pryor/cocaine/dick metaphor — the extended explanation that follows makes the setup even more committed: Pam Grier, cocaine cervix, delayed orgasm. New York City = cocaine-dipped penis.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout comedic monologue of the episode. The Richard Pryor/Pam Grier/cocaine-on-cervix detail is presented as established historical fact (it likely is), and Ilana's extension of the metaphor through to 'one giant delayed orgasm' is genuinely inspired. The concept — New York as a substance that numbs you so you endure longer — is both a good joke AND a real observation about why people choose difficult cities. The economy score suffers because it runs long, but the originality and character integration are exceptional. No one but Ilana would arrive at this metaphor. High quotability — this is the line someone posts on Tumblr. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in sophistication.

jokes.text: Ilana pivots from the cosmic NYC metaphor to: 'Oh, an open house. We should go tomorrow. I bet they'll have free cookies.' / Abbi: 'Yeah, I bet they'll have a free toilet! What? I have to pee.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just delivered a profound/absurd philosophy of New York City suffering
jokes.punchline: Hard cut to the most practical, small-scale concerns imaginable — free cookies, a toilet. The contrast deflates the grandeur immediately.
jokes.explanation: The deflation from cosmic to bodily is a classic Broad City structural move. Ilana philosophizes, then immediately wants free cookies. Abbi's 'I have to pee' is the most human possible response to a five-minute discussion about the meaning of urban life. The show earns its raunchy philosophy by consistently grounding it in the physical.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana discover a huge, sunny Florida apartment with a kitchen 'that's its own room' — Abbi: 'Oh, a home, we don't have those. We have New York City apartments.'
jokes.setup: They tour a spacious Florida retirement community apartment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's flat categorization — 'we don't have homes, we have New York City apartments' — treats this as an established anthropological distinction
jokes.explanation: Relatable NYC observation delivered with good economy. The framing of 'apartments' vs 'homes' as categorical difference rather than spectrum is the good structural move here.

jokes.text: Ilana lists the amenities: 'A lanai, prune juice on tap. Life Alert buttons in every corner.'
jokes.setup: Abbi rhapsodizes about the apartment's features
jokes.punchline: Ilana adds the old-people amenities as though they're equally appealing — 'prune juice on tap' and 'Life Alert buttons' as selling points, not warnings
jokes.explanation: The specific trio of retirement amenities works as a list joke — each one is more specifically elderly than the last. 'Prune juice on tap' is the best one because the 'on tap' implies a pipeline infrastructure dedicated to prune juice.

jokes.text: The apartment costs $425 a month. Ilana's mental math: '425 a night, yeah, times 30 nights, that's $12,000 a month.' Realtor: 'No, $425 a month.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks the price; the realtor says $425
jokes.punchline: Ilana's automatic assumption that it must be per-night, and her confident math in arriving at $12,000 — a number so NYC-calibrated that $425/month doesn't register as possible
jokes.explanation: A great structural gag about NYC-calibrated minds. The humor is in Ilana showing her work — she doesn't just assume, she calculates. And the math is correct for her assumption. The realtor's flat correction is the punchline.

jokes.text: Simultaneous responses to moving in: Abbi says 'I could see us living here...' while Ilana says 'Dying here.'
jokes.setup: They've both decided they want the apartment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's optimistic 'living here' vs Ilana's honest 'dying here' — in a retirement community, the latter is technically more accurate
jokes.explanation: Tight two-word inversion. 'Dying here' is delivered as equally enthusiastic as 'living here,' which is the whole joke — Ilana is not being morbid, she genuinely means this is a place to end your days, and she's fine with it. The dark/literal reading layered onto the enthusiastic delivery.

jokes.text: The realtor's long pause before offering: 'But...why don't you go ahead and apply?' — implying he knows they don't qualify but is drawn to them anyway
jokes.setup: The realtor has just informed them there's another couple 'the right age' who's already interested
jokes.punchline: The silence and then the 'But...' — a man breaking his own professional judgment because these two women have somehow charmed him
jokes.explanation: The pause is funnier than the line. The realtor is essentially acknowledging that these women are under 55 and not eligible but he's going to let them apply anyway — his helplessness is the joke. Competent beat.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I don't wanna rise and grind anymore. I wanna rise and then, like, lay right back down.'
jokes.setup: They're discussing whether to actually move to Florida
jokes.punchline: The deflation of 'rise and grind' culture into its opposite — 'rise and lay right back down' — as an honest aspiration
jokes.explanation: Clean inversion of a cliché. 'Rise and grind' is exhausted as a phrase; the response 'rise and lay right back down' is a good subversion that feels true for these characters. High quotability because it articulates a millennial aspiration with genuine economy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'it is net-twerking time, gaga'
jokes.setup: They've decided to charm the retirement community residents to get the apartment
jokes.punchline: 'Net-twerking' — networking + twerking collapsed into one word, with 'gaga' as an affectionate Florida-appropriate diminutive
jokes.explanation: A genuinely good portmanteau. 'Net-twerking' works as a concept — networking with your body/personality rather than business cards — and it's entirely consistent with how Ilana approaches every social situation. The word 'gaga' (grandmother) as a term of endearment for the old people they're about to charm adds a layer. High character integration because only Ilana coins words like this.

jokes.text: Edna and Ethel's tennis match: 'That was out! I win, bitch!' / 'Oh, shove it, Edna.'
jokes.setup: Two elderly women are playing tennis; Abbi and Ilana watch, trying to figure out who has more community power
jokes.punchline: Edna's completely unsporting 'I win, bitch!' delivered presumably with full conviction — these are not gentle grandmothers
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between expectation (sweet old ladies playing tennis) and reality (savage competitiveness). 'I win, bitch!' is excellent because it's not a mean thing to say; it's a victorious thing to say, which is somehow more alarming from an elderly woman. 'Shove it, Edna' as the response establishes that this is their normal register.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'She's like a cyberbully IRL.' / Ilana: 'So, like a bully.' / Abbi: 'Huh. I forgot about bullies.'
jokes.setup: Ilana calls Ethel a 'cyberbully IRL'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's deadpan deconstruction ('so, like a bully') and Abbi's genuine, slight wonder at the concept of bullies as if she'd suppressed the memory
jokes.explanation: This three-line sequence is among the episode's best structured exchanges. Ilana's correction of her own metaphor is smart; Abbi's 'Huh. I forgot about bullies' is the perfect button — implying that a bully is so anachronistic in their New York bubble that it's a forgotten concept. The delivery timing is everything, and the structure (setup/reversal/button) is textbook. High economy: three lines, three distinct comic beats.

jokes.text: Ilana assessing Ethel: 'She's like a cyberbully IRL.' Abbi: 'So, like a bully.' Ilana: 'Huh. I forgot about bullies.'
jokes.setup: They're analyzing which power player to court
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'I forgot about bullies' — the implication that the internet age has made in-person bullying so rare in her consciousness that it's a historical artifact
jokes.explanation: A three-beat joke that ends on a genuinely surprising observation. Beat 1: Ilana maps digital behavior onto physical reality. Beat 2: Abbi points out that this is just a bully. Beat 3: Ilana's genuine realization that the word 'bully' is something she's forgotten. 'I forgot about bullies' is an exceptional line because it accidentally says something true about how internet-native culture has renamed and remapped everything — including things that predate the internet. High originality because this is an observation that hasn't been made this way before. Strong claim on 8+ with the Edna/bully three-beat structure.

jokes.text: Old woman tells Ilana she 'got to [BLEEP] JFK' so Ethel didn't have to
jokes.setup: The older woman is explaining why she owes her friend — she took one for the team
jokes.punchline: 'I got to [BLEEP] JFK and she didn't' — sleeping with John F. Kennedy as a sacrifice made for a friend's benefit, framed with complete casualness
jokes.explanation: A spectacular non-sequitur that also feels historically plausible. The joke operates on multiple levels: (1) the sheer casualness of the JFK claim, (2) it's framed as a selfless act done for a friend ('so she didn't have to'), (3) Ilana's reaction is 'Ooh, such a predator' — which applies the modern language of misconduct to the most powerful man of the era with perfect comic anachronism. High originality because this specific joke (JFK sex as favor-owed debt between old women) is not something you encounter elsewhere.

jokes.text: Ilana's response to the JFK revelation: 'Ooh, such a predator. I'm so sorry. Women have been treated horrible up until this time and including this time.'
jokes.setup: An elderly woman has casually described sleeping with JFK as something she did 'so her friend didn't have to'
jokes.punchline: Ilana translates this through a contemporary feminist lens — 'such a predator' applied to JFK, then the sweeping 'up until this time and including this time'
jokes.explanation: The joke is Ilana doing rapid ideological processing — she knows the right framework (predatory power dynamics) and applies it to a 60-year-old anecdote from an elderly woman who told it without any sense of complaint. 'Up until this time and including this time' is the best part — a slightly absurd formulation that adds comedy through its awkward comprehensiveness.

jokes.text: Eliot scatting / improvising music alone in the apartment [10:11-10:22 scene]
jokes.setup: Eliot has been having a terrible day — lost luggage, grandmother's swimsuit, stuck on hold
jokes.punchline: Cut to Eliot alone, scatting to himself — a private moment of self-entertainment that implies he's found a way to cope, and it's this
jokes.explanation: A quick visual character beat. Eliot's scatting as private stress relief is a small, specific detail that tells us who he is. The comedy is in catching someone in a very specific private behavior. Not a major joke but a solid texture beat.

jokes.text: Eliot's airline hold situation: transferred from 'misplaced baggage' to 'lost baggage' — 'What, no, how are those different?'
jokes.setup: Eliot has been on hold for two hours trying to find his lost luggage
jokes.punchline: The airline distinguishes between 'misplaced' and 'lost' as separate departments — a bureaucratic distinction that makes no difference to Eliot
jokes.explanation: Competent airline bureaucracy joke. Functional and relatable but not a particularly fresh observation — the absurdity of corporate departmentalization is familiar comedic territory. Gets a laugh from the situation more than the writing.

jokes.text: Bobbi asks Eliot to 'thump' her back: 'Why don't you make yourself useful and either help me pack or thump me? I'm having a very bad day.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is frustrated after arguing with Bev; Eliot walks in
jokes.punchline: 'Or thump me' — presented as an equal alternative to packing, as casual as asking someone to hand you a glass of water
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the false equivalence — pack OR thump, as if these are comparable tasks. Bobbi's matter-of-fact delivery of a request for physical impact as a valid stress relief option is characteristically Bobbi. The bad day motivator makes it feel deserved.

jokes.text: Eliot thumping Bobbi's back and her making sounds that disturb him: 'Come on, deeper. Right there, ah, right there. Ahh...' / Eliot: 'Mom... The noises, the noises.'
jokes.setup: Eliot is reluctantly thumping his mother's back as requested
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's moaning escalates to the point where Eliot breaks: 'The noises, the noises.' The sounds are apparently deeply unseemly.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from medical-adjacent request to something that sounds sexual is classic cringe comedy. Eliot's horror at his own mother's sounds — 'The noises, the noises' (the repetition is important) — is perfectly calibrated. High character integration: only this mother/son pair could arrive at this dynamic.

jokes.text: Bobbi and Eliot mutually [BLEEP] each other — 'All right, [BLEEP] you.' / '[BLEEP] you!' / 'Sorry.'
jokes.setup: Eliot stops thumping because of the noises; Bobbi is ungrateful
jokes.punchline: They both curse at each other, then both immediately say 'Sorry' — the aggression and the repair happening in the same breath
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat that captures the specific cadence of a loving-but-chaotic family relationship. The simultaneous 'sorry' is the key — it shows they're well-practiced in this routine. Economical and affectionate even in its aggression.

jokes.text: Ilana raises the political concern about living in Florida: 'Donald [BLEEP] land' — then the text from Jaime: 'It is four degrees in New York City. And our pipes in our apartment just froze and burst... again.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is expressing reservations about Florida's politics
jokes.punchline: Jaime's text arrives with catastrophic NYC news — 'again' implying this is a recurring disaster. The political concerns immediately give way to survival concerns.
jokes.explanation: The structural joke here is excellent — the ideological argument is immediately undercut by material reality. Abbi's 'This is a good decision, it's great' (a complete reversal) right after the text is a perfect structure. The word 'again' is doing a lot of comedic work — suggesting the pipes burst regularly.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Let's go borrow Grandma Esther's dope Cadillac. Head up to "Pube-icks," AKA Publix.'
jokes.setup: They're planning their social strategy for the board vote
jokes.punchline: 'Pube-icks' as a deliberate mispronunciation of Publix — crude, juvenile, and completely unnecessary; Ilana delivers it with complete commitment
jokes.explanation: A lowbrow wordplay gag that works entirely on Ilana's commitment. 'Pube-icks' is childish but the delivery as if it's a normal thing to say elevates it slightly. The 'AKA Publix' clarification is necessary and also funny because she knows she needs to translate herself.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Florida might be filled with [BLEEP] supporters, but if we move here, our votes could swing the state.'
jokes.setup: They're rationalizing why moving to a politically hostile state is actually okay
jokes.punchline: Ilana's activist spin — reframing moving to a retirement community to get cheap rent as a political act that could swing Florida. The self-serving logic is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Character-perfect rationalization. Only Ilana would immediately recast selfish apartment-hunting as political resistance. The joke also lands because it's not entirely wrong — two liberal votes in a swing county would technically matter. The earnestness makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Ilana calls Publix 'Pube-icks'
jokes.setup: Suggesting they go to Publix for food for the community meeting
jokes.punchline: The deliberate mispronunciation that emphasizes 'pube' — Ilana cannot resist making any word sexual if there's even a marginal phonetic opportunity
jokes.explanation: A quick low-hanging pun that works because it's consistent with Ilana's character. Not highbrow, not original as a concept, but character-integrated and delivered with commitment. Functional.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana dancing in Grandma Esther's Cadillac on the way to Publix [12:10-12:44 musical sequence]
jokes.setup: They've made their decision; they're driving to get food for the board meeting
jokes.punchline: The extended dance sequence in a retiree's Cadillac — the visual of two twentysomethings twerking in a grandma's car to hip-hop while in a retirement community
jokes.explanation: A joyful visual sequence that isn't so much a joke as a comedic mood. The contrast (elderly Cadillac, young women, hip-hop) is the pleasure. Broad City frequently uses music-and-movement sequences as comedic punctuation. It works as tone and character even if it's not a structured joke.

jokes.text: Abbi cheerfully distributing hors d'oeuvres at the board meeting: 'Hope you take as much as you like. Just remember to vote for us.'
jokes.setup: They're trying to charm the retirement community board
jokes.punchline: Abbi drops the pretense mid-sentence — 'Just remember to vote for us' tacked onto a hospitality line with no apparent awareness that this is bribery
jokes.explanation: Abbi's complete transparency about what she's doing — the thin pretense of hospitality immediately abandoned — is funny because she's clearly doing what she was told not to do, but she can't help herself. Small joke but well-timed.

jokes.text: Ilana's deadpan observational: 'Bitches are horny for butterscotch.'
jokes.setup: Watching elderly residents enthusiastically consume the candy/snacks they've brought
jokes.punchline: The blunt anthropological observation — 'horny for butterscotch' applies the language of desire to elderly candy enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'Horny for butterscotch' achieves maximum economy — five words, extraordinary comedy. The word 'horny' applied to candy enthusiasm is unexpected but instantly visual. The word 'bitches' applied to elderly women by someone who clearly adores them is affectionately subversive. This is the kind of line that gets screenshot-shared. High originality — this specific phrasing for this specific observation hasn't been said this way before. Strong economy score (9.5 — near-perfect word economy). Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in punch-per-word ratio.

jokes.text: The board takes approximately five seconds to reach a decision after being told it will 'confer'
jokes.setup: The board announces it will now 'confer' — implying a deliberative process
jokes.punchline: Ilana: 'I wonder how long they'll take.' Board: 'We've reached a decision.' — immediately
jokes.explanation: A clean structural gag — the pomposity of 'The Board will now confer' deflated by instant resolution. The humor is in the gap between the ceremony and the speed. Ilana's 'I wonder how long...' being immediately answered is tight comedic timing.

jokes.text: The board president's acceptance speech: 'Well, we'd rather have two young Jewish [BLEEP] than a couple of [BLEEP].'
jokes.setup: The board congratulates Abbi and Ilana and welcomes them to the community
jokes.punchline: The warmth is immediately complicated by the explanation, which reveals casual bigotry dressed as a compliment — the audience applauds, making it more uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: A deeply uncomfortable joke that the show earns through setup. The whole episode has been building toward the reality of who these Florida residents are — the guns, the 'Stand Your Ground' reference, the whiteness observations. The board's 'compliment' crystallizes all of that into a single moment. The applause from the other residents deepens the cringe. The structure (warm acceptance → bigoted explanation → continued applause) is excellent and the show is making a pointed observation about whose company feels 'safe.' High impact because the audience laughs and then immediately feels bad about it.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana's immediate reversal: 'We gotta get the hell outta here!' / 'I don't even know what to do with this!' / 'I can't believe guns come with the apartment!'
jokes.setup: They just got accepted into the community
jokes.punchline: The instant reversal — they flee the community they spent the whole episode trying to join, having realized what it actually is
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff of the entire Florida arc. The joke is the gap between what they wanted (escape from NYC) and what they'd be escaping INTO. 'I can't believe guns come with the apartment!' is a great button — it plays back all the gun humor from the episode and lands on the specific absurdity of apartment-included firearms as the final dealbreaker.

jokes.text: Bobbi and Bev's negotiation: 'Oh, please, people pay not to sleep on that pile-a-bullshit.' / 'That shit is Ikea. Do I look like a frickin' idiot? I taught you everything you know, girlie.'
jokes.setup: Sisters negotiating who gets what from Grandma Esther's estate
jokes.punchline: Bev calling out the fake Van Gogh and Ikea china as junk — and Bobbi's 'I taught you everything you know' as a response to being caught trying to pass off Ikea as valuable china
jokes.explanation: The Ikea-as-china gag is specific and funny (the specificity of Ikea as opposed to any generic cheap furniture). But the best beat is Bobbi's pivot — called out for trying to trick her sister, she immediately claims that Bev's savviness is something she TAUGHT her, making her own attempted deception into a source of pride.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'All right, you let me have the ring. And I'm gonna give you the end tables and the sleeper sofa.' / Bev: 'Oh, please, people pay not to sleep on that pile-a-bullshit.'
jokes.setup: The sisters are now negotiating the estate in calmer tones
jokes.punchline: Bev's rejection of the sleeper sofa — 'people pay not to sleep on that pile-a-bullshit' — is specific and vivid; the exact reversal of Bobbi's framing (she presents it as a gift, Bev identifies it as an anti-gift)
jokes.explanation: 'People pay not to sleep on that pile-a-bullshit' is a genuinely funny construction — the idea that the sofa has negative value, that its presence is something you'd pay to avoid. Bev's sharpness here matches Bobbi's, revealing they're evenly matched.

jokes.text: Bobbi threatens to exclude Bev from the annual Sandals vacation as a negotiating chip: 'maybe you shouldn't come to Sandals this year.'
jokes.setup: The negotiation is heating up; Bobbi needs leverage
jokes.punchline: Sandals as the nuclear option — she could have threatened anything, but the yearly all-inclusive Caribbean resort vacation is apparently the most precious thing in the relationship
jokes.explanation: A great character joke. The choice of Sandals as the ultimate threat tells us everything about these two women — they are simultaneously petty about a grandmother's fake Van Gogh and deeply committed to a yearly couple's resort trip. 'How dare you take my Sandals vacay hostage' is a phenomenal line. The word 'hostage' applied to a vacation is precise and funny. High character integration because this is specifically a thing two women of this generation and cultural background would threaten.

jokes.text: Bev's response: 'How dare you take my Sandals vacay hostage. How dare you!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi has just threatened to cut Bev out of the Sandals trip
jokes.punchline: 'Vacay hostage' — treating a vacation exclusion as a geopolitical crisis; the repetition of 'How dare you' with genuine outrage
jokes.explanation: 'Vacay hostage' is quotable as a compound noun. The outrage is proportionate to the stakes within the characters' worldview. The doubling of 'How dare you' (pause, then again) is a classic comedy beat — the first is the punch, the second confirms she means it.

jokes.text: The airline finds Eliot's bag right after he says 'Yeah, whatever' and hangs up — he gets the news immediately after giving up
jokes.setup: Eliot has been on hold for hours; finally reaching someone who says they'll dispatch the bag
jokes.punchline: Eliot's defeated 'Yeah, whatever' + phone thud — the joke being that he gets resolution right when he's abandoned all hope. The phone thud suggests he can't even muster enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: The running gag payoff. The resolution coming after he's given up is ironic in a satisfying way, but the 'phone thud' is the actual joke — the sound of a man who has been through too much to even feel relieved. Competent closing beat for a running gag.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Even the type of fish salad they like is white.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are processing the racist acceptance speech
jokes.punchline: The whitefish salad they brought as a goodwill offering now reads as an accidental symbol — 'even the fish salad is white' extends the racism observation into absurd food semiotics

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Dude, shaming other white people for their racism is, like, my thing. I've been in a haze!'
jokes.setup: Abbi is having her racial awakening about the community
jokes.punchline: Ilana's complaint is not about the racism per se but about being beaten to the white-guilt performance — 'that's my thing' — and her self-recrimination for missing it ('I've been in a haze!')
jokes.explanation: A genuinely sharp self-aware joke about performative woke-ness. Ilana's framing of anti-racism as 'her thing' — a personal brand she maintains — is both a character joke and a pointed observation about a specific type of progressive white person. The 'I've been in a haze' self-flagellation is the kicker. High character integration — this is specifically Ilana's specific brand of well-intentioned self-centeredness. This is comparable to the 8.0 anchor because it's a sharp cultural observation with genuine character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Get me back to New York where everybody hates Donald [BLEEP] and Mike Pence.'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are cataloguing everything wrong with Florida
jokes.punchline: New York reframed positively as a place where the collective hatred is at least well-directed — shared hatred as a bonding community value
jokes.explanation: A solid political joke that works because it reframes something normally negative (a culture of hate) as a virtue — as long as it's the right hate. Competent, character-appropriate, but not original enough for a higher score.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'What are we? In a goddamn Woody Allen movie?'
jokes.setup: They're listing the absence of diversity: no gay people, no interracial couples, only straight white people
jokes.punchline: Woody Allen as the cultural shorthand for a world populated exclusively by straight white people — with the added layer that Woody Allen is also being implicitly criticized
jokes.explanation: An efficient cultural reference that earns its place because it's apt — Woody Allen's NYC films are genuinely noted for their lack of diversity. The question form ('What are we, in a...') is a stronger structure than a statement. The reference also lands differently post-Woody Allen allegations (this episode is 2017), adding a layer.

jokes.text: The weed-smoking scene: Bobbi and Bev finally reconcile over a shared joint outside Grandma Esther's apartment
jokes.setup: The sisters have been fighting viciously all episode
jokes.punchline: The visual of these two bickering older women sharing a joint and immediately mellowing — a classic comedy trope but placed at exactly the right moment after peak conflict
jokes.explanation: The structural placement is the comedy — the visual of two older women passing a joint is not original, but it's a well-earned beat after the sisters' escalating argument. The storytelling function is strong even if the joke itself isn't inspired.

jokes.text: High Bobbi recounts stealing Grandma Esther's speed in the Catskills — 'And we're trying to hide it from Grandma. But she spotted us immediately 'cause she was high on speed herself'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is telling a fond memory about the Catskills bungalow, apparently a coming-of-age story
jokes.punchline: Grandma Esther was also on her own speed — she caught them not through parental wisdom but because she was high and thus equally alert
jokes.explanation: A great story joke with a strong structural turn. The expected beat would be that Grandma's maternal instinct caught them; the actual reason is that she was also medicated and therefore equally wired. The specificity of 'diet pills — speed, you know' sets up the punchline perfectly. Also establishes Grandma Esther as a character in retrospect, making the whole episode about her richer. High earned-vs-cheap score because the punchline comes from narrative logic, not randomness.

jokes.text: Bobbi corrects Bev about losing her virginity: Bev insists it was 'Jerry Katz the year after I went to college' — Bev: 'Unh-unh, I lied.'
jokes.setup: The stoned sisters are reminiscing; Bobbi confidently 'corrects' Bev about her own virginity story
jokes.punchline: Bev: 'I lied.' — she maintained a false virginity story for decades
jokes.explanation: The setup relies on Bobbi being confidently wrong about her sister's private history — which is itself a character joke. 'I lied' as a two-word reveal, delivered stoned, 50 years after the fact, is excellent. The beat that follows (the details of the actual first time) escalates the joke further.

jokes.text: Bev's virginity story: she lost it to a boy with a scoliosis brace, and 'I was so petrified that I was gonna wake all of youse up because it kept bangin' against the headboard.'
jokes.setup: Bev reveals she lost her virginity at 15 in the bungalow to Neil Friedman
jokes.punchline: Neil Friedman had a scoliosis brace, and Bev's fear was not about getting caught having sex but about the brace hitting the headboard and waking people up
jokes.explanation: The scoliosis brace detail is what makes this exceptional. It's a completely unexpected, highly specific piece of information that generates a very specific comic image. Two layers: (1) the brace as a comedy-of-the-body detail (scoliosis braces banging against headboards), (2) Bev's concern being about waking people up rather than being discovered having sex — a very 15-year-old priority. The follow-up punchline 'Bangin'?' from Bobbi is a perfect button — she catches the double meaning. Then Bobbi immediately drops the ring dispute. This is the episode's best comedic story beat and one of the best pieces of writing in the half-hour. Close to the Snip-Snap anchor in terms of unexpected specificity and character revelation through a physical/medical detail.

jokes.text: Bev: 'Wait a minute, Neil Friedman? Wasn't he the one with the scoliosis brace?' / Bev: 'Uh-huh.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi tries to place Neil Friedman in her memory.
jokes.punchline: Neil Friedman is remembered specifically for his scoliosis brace — and yes, that's the one.
jokes.explanation: The scoliosis brace detail is perfect specificity — it's exactly the kind of thing that would get remembered about someone from a Catskills summer 50 years ago. The quiet 'Uh-huh' confirmation from Bev — no embarrassment, no explanation — is funnier than any defense would be.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'I was so petrified that I was gonna wake all of youse up because it kept bangin' against the headboard.' / 'Bangin'?' / Loud laughter from the sisters
jokes.setup: Bobbi is describing the scoliosis brace during the virginity-loss story
jokes.punchline: The word 'banging' applied to the brace hitting the headboard inadvertently means something else — a classic double entendre that Bobbi herself doesn't clock, or maybe does, and the laughter is the acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: The double entendre is not particularly original, but in context it works exceptionally well. The story has been building (stolen speed, secret affair, scoliosis brace) and 'banging' against the headboard lands hard because of all the earned context. The sisters' shared laughter is contagious and the impact score reflects the moment's cumulative power.

jokes.text: Bobbi's response: 'Bangin'?' — catching the double meaning, then immediately deciding to give Bev the ring
jokes.setup: Bev has described the scoliosis brace 'banging against the headboard'
jokes.punchline: Bobbi's incredulous/delighted 'Bangin'?' — she catches both meanings at once — and then the ring dispute is immediately resolved
jokes.explanation: One word — maximum comedy and narrative function. 'Bangin'?' simultaneously: (1) picks up the double entendre, (2) expresses delighted disbelief, (3) serves as the pivot that resolves the entire ring conflict. The show resolves a conflict not through sentiment or reason but through laughter at a shared filthy story. This is structurally elegant. Economy score is near-perfect because single syllable achieves everything.

jokes.text: Bobbi: 'Beverly, you know what? You take the ring.' / Bev: 'No, Bobbi, you take it.' / Bobbi: 'No, you take it, Bev. Who could care? It's just a material possession.'
jokes.setup: The ring dispute that defined the sisters' conflict
jokes.punchline: The pivot from obsessive conflict to total mutual dismissal — 'Who could care? It's just a material possession' — resolved not by mediation but by weed and a good story

jokes.text: Eliot: 'Oh, my God, Mom, you need to smoke more often, like, every damn day.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi has just become mellow and generous after getting stoned
jokes.punchline: Eliot's prescription for his mother's behavior: daily weed — delivered with complete sincerity by a son who has clearly suffered from sober-Bobbi for decades
jokes.explanation: A sincere compliment-as-criticism. Eliot's 'every damn day' is the key — not 'occasionally' but constantly. The implication that sober Bobbi is so difficult that daily medication is recommended is funny and specific.

jokes.text: Stoned Bobbi's Facebook epiphany: 'Sometimes I wanna be one of those people that just, like, deletes Facebook altogether. And then I can always tell everyone I deleted Facebook. But then I'd miss all the baby pics.'
jokes.setup: High Bobbi is now discussing Abbi's profile picture again
jokes.punchline: The motivation for deleting Facebook is the social credit of having deleted it — but the baby pics win. An accurate portrait of a certain type of social media ambivalence.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the opening Facebook joke lands better here because Bobbi is stoned and self-aware. The insight — that people delete Facebook primarily to announce it — is accurate and lands well. The baby pics exception is the character-specific detail that grounds it.

jokes.text: Bobbi reveals, while stoned: 'I saw earlier on Facebook that Lincoln changed his status to single.'
jokes.setup: They're all high, having a warm moment. Bobbi is being unusually forthcoming.
jokes.punchline: Bobbi has been monitoring Ilana's ex-boyfriend's Facebook status and chooses THIS moment to share — adding 'I wasn't gonna say anything. But now I'm stoned.'
jokes.explanation: A multi-callback joke. Bobbi's Facebook surveillance of Ilana's friends (established in joke 9) pays off here — she's been watching Lincoln's status. The 'I wasn't gonna say anything. But now I'm stoned.' is an excellent character line — the weed as truth serum. And the revelation itself is emotionally loaded (Ilana's reaction: 'Cool. Cool.' with a beat) making this both funny and genuinely moving. Emotionally smart comedy.

jokes.text: Ilana's response to Lincoln's single status: 'Cool. Cool.' [beat]
jokes.setup: Bobbi has just revealed Lincoln changed his relationship status
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Cool. Cool.' — two syllables trying to perform nonchalance over what is clearly not nonchalance. The repeated 'Cool' is the tell.
jokes.explanation: Two words that carry an entire emotional arc. The double 'Cool' is not deadpan — it's deadpan trying to cover for not-cool. Ilana's character is defined by exuberance; when she goes quiet, the comedy is in the silence. This is the episode's emotional center expressed through understatement, and the craft is exceptional. High economy (9.5) because the joke achieves maximum emotional payload with two words. Matches the emotional sophistication of the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Ilana reveals she brought weed 'in my pussy' on the plane — Bobbi coughs / Abbi: 'Ew.' Bobbi: 'I knew it, it's a thing.' / Bev: 'Oh, big freakin' deal. She came out of you. You can't smoke a little weed that came out of her?'
jokes.setup: Bobbi asks where Ilana got the pot; Ilana answers without ceremony
jokes.punchline: The escalation: Ilana's casual 'in my pussy' → Bobbi's coughing fit → Abbi's 'Ew' → Bobbi's 'I knew it, it's a thing' (implying she had suspicions) → Bev's completely unfazed 'you can't smoke a little weed that came out of her?'
jokes.explanation: A five-character comedic sequence where every single beat lands differently. The structure is exceptional: (1) Ilana's flat delivery of something shocking, (2) Abbi's disgust, (3) Bobbi's strange 'I knew it' implying prior knowledge of this practice, (4) Bev's grandmotherly pragmatism ('she came out of you') as the trump card. Bev's logic — that maternal origin makes all subsequent physical substances acceptable — is both absurd and somehow unassailable. High character integration: each character's reaction is perfectly specific to them. This is among the best-constructed multi-beat jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Bobbi's goodbye to the apartment: 'You gave us some fun times and E.coli from the pool that one year.'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is saying goodbye to Grandma Esther's apartment
jokes.punchline: The E. coli detail — sandwiched between warmth and nostalgia as an equal memory alongside 'fun times'
jokes.explanation: The specificity of E. coli (not just 'we got sick,' but specifically E. coli from the pool) makes this land. The equal weight given to E. coli and 'fun times' — presented as a balanced eulogy — is dark but affectionate. The 'that one year' implies there was only one year of E. coli, which is oddly reassuring.

jokes.text: Bobbi to the grandchildren on departure: 'Use condoms!'
jokes.setup: Bobbi is saying goodbye to Abbi and Ilana
jokes.punchline: Standard maternal parting advice — except Bobbi's version is sexual safety, delivered with the same warmth as 'call me when you land'
jokes.explanation: Brief but character-perfect. Bobbi's brand of maternal care is expressed entirely through contraceptive advice — a callback to her establishing the diaphragm line. Two words that feel earned by everything we know about her.

jokes.text: Eliot: 'Thanks for the full new wardrobe.' — as he leaves in Grandma Esther's clothes
jokes.setup: Eliot's luggage was lost the entire trip
jokes.punchline: He leaves wearing Grandma Esther's clothes and is making the best of it — sarcastic gratitude that is also genuine, since he did end up with a 'wardrobe'
jokes.explanation: A clean payoff for the luggage running gag. Eliot's acceptance of wearing a dead grandmother's clothes is funny because it's delivered with complete composure — he's done fighting. The 'full wardrobe' framing is deadpan but technically accurate.
